does: SUPREME is a multi-GPU framework for evaluating machine unlearning methods in image classification. The framework distributes the computationally expensive pipeline of model training, unlearning, and evaluation across multiple GPUs to enable rapid evaluation across multiple random seeds. It provides a registry-based architecture that allows researchers to easily add new unlearning methods, evaluation metrics, model architectures, and experimental scenarios. The authors demonstrate the framework on the Pins Face Recognition dataset using ResNet18 and Vision Transformer models under both full-class and random-sample unlearning settings, evaluating performance across ten seeds to establish reproducibility and statistical reliability.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature on machine unlearning, which addresses the challenge of removing the influence of specific training examples from trained models without expensive retraining from scratch. The paper extends prior single-GPU unlearning frameworks by introducing distributed computing infrastructure, drawing on parallel training practices common in large-scale deep learning. It responds to practical bottlenecks in evaluating unlearning methods rigorously, as existing approaches make statistical assessment across multiple seeds infeasible due to computational constraints. The framework synthesizes recent work on unlearning metrics and methods while adapting software engineering practices like registry-based design patterns from the broader machine learning systems community.
enables: SUPREME enables more rigorous and reproducible evaluation of unlearning methods by reducing the computational barrier to multi-seed evaluation. The framework's modular, registry-based design facilitates systematic comparison of competing unlearning approaches and metrics, supporting the development of standardized benchmarks in this emerging area. By lowering computational costs, the work opens pathways for researchers to explore unlearning at larger scales and with more diverse model architectures and datasets. The distributed architecture could support future work on evaluating unlearning under more complex scenarios, such as federated unlearning or continuous data deletion, and potentially enable adoption of unlearning evaluation in real-world applications where reproducibility and statistical soundness are critical requirements.
